explicit_text: I was born in East Los Angeles on July 25, 1912, in a section then called Belvedere. Growing up, East Los Angeles was a developing area just east of the city limits of Los Angeles, which stopped at Indiana Street. It was a place where my family lived and where I attended schools like Garfield High School after junior high. East Los Angeles has always been closely tied to my early life and education.

explicit_text: Belvedere was the name of the area where I was born and raised, just east of Indiana Street and outside the city limits of Los Angeles. It was an unincorporated area in Los Angeles County before it became part of what is now called East Los Angeles. I lived at 225 South Herbert Street in Belvedere and attended Belvedere Elementary and Junior High Schools there. Belvedere was a close-knit community during my childhood.

explicit_text: The Janss Investment Company was a major developer responsible for subdividing Belvedere Gardens and later developing Westwood, Los Angeles. They also donated large acreage to the University of California, Los Angeles. Janss played a significant role in shaping the neighborhoods around where I grew up, including Burbank, which they may have developed earlier. Their work influenced the growth of the areas I knew well as a child.

explicit_text: My mother, Martha Bohmke, came to the United States from Germany when she was sixteen years old. She arrived not knowing the language and stayed with her older sister in Chicago before eventually settling in California. She was entirely a homemaker and placed great importance on learning a trade, which influenced me when I found my interest in printing. Her background and values shaped much of my upbringing.

explicit_text: Robert was my oldest brother, but sadly he died of the flu when he was only nine years old. He was the firstborn in our family, preceding Theodore, myself, and our younger brother Carl. His early death was a significant loss for our family during my childhood. I remember him as part of my family history.

explicit_text: Chester L. Bond was my printing teacher at Belvedere Junior High School. He was a Seventh-day Adventist who had worked at the Adventist publishing plant in Mountain View near Palo Alto. His interest and teaching inspired me to become firmly attached to printing. He was an excellent printer and a significant influence during my early vocational education.
